annotations.label: Bézier curve

anchor.snippet: is a parametric curve used in computer graphics and related fields

annotations.note: Mathlib has no Bézier curve definition (only the underlying Bernstein polynomials).

annotations.label: Control points define the curve

anchor.snippet: defines a smooth, continuous curve by means of a formula

annotations.note: No Bézier curve construction from control points exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Order of a Bézier curve

anchor.snippet: where n is called the order of the curve

annotations.note: Bézier curves and their order are not formalized.

annotations.label: Endpoints are first and last control points

anchor.snippet: The first and last control points are always the endpoints of the curve

annotations.note: Bernstein basis evaluation at 0/1 (eval_at_0, eval_at_1) underlies B(0)=P₀, B(1)=Pₙ, but Bézier curves themselves are absent.

annotations.label: Linear Bézier curve

anchor.snippet: a linear Bézier curve is simply a line between those two points

annotations.note: `AffineMap.lineMap p₀ p₁` is exactly the linear Bézier curve, generalized to any affine space.

annotations.label: Linear Bézier equals linear interpolation

anchor.snippet: is equivalent to linear interpolation

annotations.note: lineMap_apply_module states lineMap p₀ p₁ c = (1-c)•p₀ + c•p₁, i.e. linear interpolation.

annotations.label: Quadratic Bézier curve

anchor.snippet: A quadratic Bézier curve is the path traced by the function

annotations.note: The degree-2 Bernstein basis is formalized but the quadratic Bézier curve is not defined.

annotations.label: Tangents intersect at P1

anchor.snippet: the tangents to the curve at P 0 and P 2 intersect at P 1

annotations.note: This geometric tangency claim about quadratic Bézier curves is not formalized.

annotations.label: Second derivative of quadratic Bézier

anchor.snippet: The second derivative of the Bézier curve with respect to t is

annotations.note: Bernstein-polynomial derivative lemmas give the building blocks, but no Bézier second-derivative statement exists.

annotations.label: Cubic Bézier curve

anchor.snippet: in the plane or in higher-dimensional space define a cubic Bézier curve

annotations.note: The degree-3 Bernstein basis exists but the cubic Bézier curve is not defined.

annotations.label: Four points convert to a cubic Bézier

anchor.snippet: Any series of 4 distinct points can be converted to a cubic Bézier curve that goes through all 4 points in order

annotations.note: Bézier interpolation through four points is not formalized.

annotations.label: Derivative of cubic Bézier

anchor.snippet: The derivative of the cubic Bézier curve with respect to t is

annotations.note: Bernstein derivative recurrence is formalized, but the cubic Bézier derivative statement is not.

annotations.label: Tschirnhausen cubic case

anchor.snippet: the resulting cubic Bézier curve is an arc of a (scaled and translated) Tschirnhausen cubic

annotations.note: The Tschirnhausen cubic and this special case are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Recursive definition of Bézier curve

anchor.snippet: A recursive definition for the Bézier curve of degree n expresses it as a point-to-point linear combination

annotations.note: The recursive (de Casteljau) definition of Bézier curves is not formalized.

annotations.label: Explicit formula

anchor.snippet: The formula can be expressed explicitly as follows

annotations.note: The Bernstein basis used in the explicit Bézier sum is formalized, but the Bézier curve sum itself is not.

annotations.label: Explicit formula for n=5

anchor.snippet: For example, when n = 5

annotations.note: The degree-5 Bernstein basis is an instance of the general definition, but no Bézier curve example exists.

annotations.label: Bernstein basis polynomials

anchor.snippet: are known as Bernstein basis polynomials of degree n

annotations.note: `bernsteinPolynomial R n ν = (choose n ν) * X^ν * (1-X)^(n-ν)` is exactly the Bernstein basis polynomial.

annotations.label: Control points

anchor.snippet: The points P i are called control points for the Bézier curve

annotations.note: Control points as Bézier terminology are not formalized.

annotations.label: Bézier polygon

anchor.snippet: is called the Bézier polygon

annotations.note: The Bézier (control) polygon is not formalized.

annotations.label: Convex hull contains the curve

anchor.snippet: The convex hull of the Bézier polygon contains the Bézier curve

annotations.note: The Bernstein partition-of-unity (sum = 1) plus nonnegativity and Mathlib's convexHull machinery would give this, but it is not stated for Bézier curves.

annotations.label: Polynomial form

anchor.snippet: express the Bézier curve as a polynomial

annotations.note: The monomial-basis polynomial form of a Bézier curve is not formalized.

annotations.label: Endpoint interpolation property

anchor.snippet: this is the so-called endpoint interpolation property

annotations.note: Bernstein eval_at_0/eval_at_1 give the endpoint values underlying interpolation, but the Bézier property is not stated.

annotations.label: Curve is a line iff control points collinear

anchor.snippet: The curve is a line if and only if all the control points are collinear

annotations.note: Mathlib has Collinear but not this characterization for Bézier curves.

annotations.label: Tangency at endpoints

anchor.snippet: is tangent to the first and last section of the Bézier polygon

annotations.note: Endpoint tangency to the control polygon is not formalized.

annotations.label: Subdivision into subcurves

anchor.snippet: A curve can be split at any point into two subcurves

annotations.note: Bézier subdivision is not formalized.

annotations.label: Circle not exactly a Bézier curve

anchor.snippet: cannot be described exactly by a Bézier or piecewise Bézier curve

annotations.note: This impossibility result is not formalized.

annotations.label: Degree elevation equivalence

anchor.snippet: Every quadratic Bézier curve is also a cubic Bézier curve

annotations.note: Degree elevation of Bézier curves is not formalized.

annotations.label: Variation diminishing property

anchor.snippet: Bézier curves have the variation diminishing property

annotations.note: The variation diminishing property is not formalized.

annotations.label: No local control

anchor.snippet: There is no local control in degree n Bézier curves

annotations.note: This qualitative property of Bézier curves is not formalized.

annotations.label: Self-intersection or cusp possible

anchor.snippet: A Bézier curve of order higher than two may intersect itself or have a cusp

annotations.note: Self-intersection/cusp behavior of Bézier curves is not formalized.

annotations.label: Quadratic Bézier is a parabolic segment

anchor.section: Second-order curve is a parabolic segment
anchor.snippet: A quadratic Bézier curve is also a segment of a parabola

annotations.note: Parabolas and this identification are not formalized.

annotations.label: Endpoint tangents meet at control point

anchor.section: Second-order curve is a parabolic segment
anchor.snippet: Tangents to the parabola at the endpoints of the curve (A and B) intersect at its control point (C)

annotations.note: This parabola tangent construction is not formalized.

annotations.label: Vertex construction

anchor.section: Second-order curve is a parabolic segment
anchor.snippet: the tangent to the curve which is perpendicular to CD (dashed cyan line) defines its vertex (V)

annotations.note: This geometric vertex construction is not formalized.

annotations.label: Directrix construction

anchor.section: Second-order curve is a parabolic segment
anchor.snippet: the line passing through G and perpendicular to CD is the directrix (solid green)

annotations.note: Parabola directrix construction is not formalized.

annotations.label: Focus construction

anchor.section: Second-order curve is a parabolic segment
anchor.snippet: The focus (F) is at the intersection of the axis and a line passing through E and perpendicular to CD

annotations.note: Parabola focus construction is not formalized.

annotations.label: Derivative of order-n curve

anchor.snippet: The derivative for a curve of order n is

annotations.note: The Bernstein derivative recurrence underlies the order-n Bézier derivative formula, but the latter is not stated.

annotations.label: de Casteljau linear construction

anchor.snippet: B ( t ) draws a line from P 0 to P 1

annotations.note: The single linear-interpolation step is `lineMap`, but the de Casteljau algorithm is not formalized.

annotations.label: de Casteljau quadratic construction

anchor.snippet: one can construct intermediate points Q 0 and Q 1

annotations.note: The de Casteljau construction is not formalized.

annotations.label: de Casteljau higher-order construction

anchor.snippet: For cubic curves one can construct intermediate points Q 0 , Q 1 , and Q 2

annotations.note: The higher-order de Casteljau construction is not formalized.

annotations.label: Offset of cubic Bézier is 10th-order curve

anchor.snippet: the two-sided offset curve of a cubic Bézier is a 10th-order algebraic curve

annotations.note: Offset curves of Bézier curves are not formalized.

annotations.label: Offset of degree-n Bézier

anchor.snippet: the two-sided offset curve is an algebraic curve of degree 4 n − 2

annotations.note: The degree of Bézier offset curves is not formalized.

annotations.label: Degree elevation

anchor.snippet: A Bézier curve of degree n can be converted into a Bézier curve of degree n + 1 with the same shape

annotations.note: Degree elevation is not formalized.

annotations.label: Degree elevation from 2 to 3

anchor.snippet: Here is the example of increasing degree from 2 to 3

annotations.note: This degree elevation example is not formalized.

annotations.label: Repeated degree elevation vertices

anchor.snippet: After r degree elevations, the polygon R r has the vertices

annotations.note: Repeated degree elevation is not formalized.

annotations.label: Exact degree reduction condition

anchor.snippet: Degree reduction can only be done exactly when the curve in question is originally elevated from a lower degree

annotations.note: Degree reduction of Bézier curves is not formalized.

annotations.label: Rational Bézier curve

anchor.snippet: The rational Bézier curve adds adjustable weights to provide closer approximations to arbitrary shapes

annotations.note: Rational Bézier curves are not formalized.

annotations.label: Full circle from complex weights

anchor.snippet: generate a full circle with radius one

annotations.note: This rational-Bézier circle example is not formalized.

annotations.label: G1 continuity

anchor.snippet: a property called G1 continuity suffices to force the control point

annotations.note: Geometric (G1) continuity of curves is not formalized.
